question_text: The electric field at a point is defined as:
explanation: Electric field E at a point is defined as the force F experienced by a unit positive test charge q₀ placed at that point: E = F/q₀. The test charge must be vanishingly small so it doesn't disturb the source charge distribution. The field is a vector quantity with SI unit N/C or V/m.
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Force experienced by any charge at that point
Force per unit positive test charge at that point

elimination_hints.hint: The force depends on the magnitude of the test charge. The field is force per UNIT charge, making it independent of the test charge.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing total force with field (force per unit charge)

elimination_hints.hint: Work done per unit charge is the definition of electric potential (V), not electric field (E).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing electric field with electric potential

elimination_hints.hint: Charge per unit area is the definition of surface charge density (σ), not electric field.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing electric field with charge density

question_text: Electric field lines start from:
explanation: Electric field lines originate from positive charges and terminate on negative charges. They represent the direction of force that a positive test charge would experience. If there are no negative charges nearby, field lines extend to infinity.
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Positive charges and end on negative charges
Negative charges and end on positive charges
Any charge and form closed loops
Regions of low field and end at regions of high field

elimination_hints.hint: This is the reverse of the convention. Field lines indicate the direction a positive test charge would move — away from positive, toward negative.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the direction convention for field lines

elimination_hints.hint: Electrostatic field lines never form closed loops — that's a property of magnetic field lines. Electric field is conservative.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing electric field lines with magnetic field lines

elimination_hints.hint: Field lines originate and terminate on charges, not on regions of different field strength.

question_text: The SI unit of electric field intensity is equivalent to:
explanation: Electric field E = F/q has SI unit N/C (newton per coulomb). Since work = force × distance, and potential V = W/q, we get V = Ed, so E = V/d, giving units V/m (volt per metre). Both N/C and V/m are equivalent and interchangeable.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: While N/C is correct, V/m is also a valid unit for electric field (E = −dV/dr). The question asks about equivalence.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing that N/C = V/m

elimination_hints.hint: V/m is correct but N/C is equally valid. E = F/q gives N/C directly.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking only the potential-based unit is valid

elimination_hints.hint: J/C is the unit of electric potential (V), not electric field. E has an additional 1/m factor.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing potential (J/C = V) with field (V/m)

question_text: Two electric field lines can never cross each other because:
explanation: If two field lines crossed, there would be two different directions of electric field at the crossing point, which is impossible — the electric field at any point has a unique direction. This is because the field is a well-defined vector function of position.
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Charges would cancel at the crossing point
The electric field at a point must have a unique direction

elimination_hints.hint: Field lines are NOT always straight — they curve around charge distributions. This is not the reason they can't cross.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking field lines are always straight

elimination_hints.hint: Whether field lines carry energy is irrelevant to why they can't cross. The reason is about direction uniqueness.
Field lines don't represent charges — they represent the direction and strength of the field. There's no charge at a crossing point.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing field lines with charges

question_text: The electric field due to a point charge q at distance r from it varies as:
explanation: From Coulomb's law, E = kq/r² = q/(4πε₀r²). The field follows an inverse square law — it decreases as the square of the distance from the charge. At twice the distance, the field is one-fourth.
subtopic: Field due to a point charge
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: 1/r dependence applies to the electric field of an infinitely long line charge, not a point charge.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing point charge field with line charge field

elimination_hints.hint: 1/r³ dependence applies to the electric field of a dipole at large distances, not a single point charge.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing point charge field with dipole field

elimination_hints.hint: r² would mean the field increases with distance, which contradicts the fact that the field weakens as you move away from a charge.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inverting the relationship — confusing direct with inverse

question_text: Two point charges +q and −q are placed at equal distances from a point P. The electric field at P is:
explanation: This depends on the geometry. If P is on the perpendicular bisector of the line joining the charges: the field components along the bisector cancel, and the net field points from +q toward −q (parallel to the line joining the charges). If P is on the line joining them between the charges: both fields point toward −q and add up. In general, the field is NOT zero because the fields from opposite charges don't cancel (they point in the same general direction at most points).
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Non-zero and depends on the position of P
Always directed perpendicular to the line joining the charges

elimination_hints.hint: The fields from +q and −q at most points do NOT cancel because they generally point in similar directions (both have a component toward −q).
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming equal and opposite charges always produce zero field everywhere

elimination_hints.hint: This is true only on the perpendicular bisector. At other points (e.g., on the line joining the charges), the field is along that line.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overgeneralising a result valid only on the bisector

elimination_hints.hint: The field magnitude varies with position and direction relative to the charges. 2kq/r² is not a universal expression.

question_text: The electric field at the midpoint of two equal positive charges +Q separated by distance 2d is:
explanation: At the midpoint, each charge is at distance d. The field from the left charge points rightward (away from +Q), and the field from the right charge points leftward (away from +Q). Both have equal magnitude kQ/d². Since they point in opposite directions, they cancel: E_net = 0.
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: kQ/d², perpendicular to the line joining them
4kQ/d², directed along the line joining them

elimination_hints.hint: The two fields are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction (both point away from their respective +Q source). They cancel perfectly at the midpoint.
elimination_hints.misconception: Adding magnitudes instead of considering vector directions

elimination_hints.hint: There is no physical reason for a perpendicular field at the midpoint of two charges on a line. By symmetry, any net field must be along the line.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming field direction without analysis

elimination_hints.hint: 4kQ/d² would require the fields to add (same direction), but they point in opposite directions from the midpoint.

question_text: The number of electric field lines emerging from a charge of +1 C in vacuum is:
explanation: By Gauss's law, the total electric flux from a charge q is Φ = q/ε₀. For q = 1 C: Φ = 1/(8.85 × 10⁻¹²) = 1.13 × 10¹¹ N·m²/C. If we associate one field line per unit flux, the number of lines is q/ε₀ ≈ 1.13 × 10¹¹. This is conventionally written as 1/ε₀.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: 1 field line for 1 C of charge would be far too few. The number is proportional to q/ε₀, which is a very large number.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking one line per coulomb

elimination_hints.hint: 4π appears in the factor 4πε₀ in Coulomb's law, but the number of field lines from 1 C is 1/ε₀, not 4π.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing 4π factor with the field line count

elimination_hints.hint: 9 × 10⁹ is the Coulomb constant k = 1/(4πε₀), not 1/ε₀. The number of lines is 1/ε₀ = 4πk ≈ 1.13 × 10¹¹.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using k instead of 1/ε₀

question_text: An electron is placed in a uniform electric field of 2 × 10⁴ N/C directed upward. The force on the electron is:
explanation: Force on charge q in field E: F = qE. For electron: F = eE = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 2 × 10⁴ = 3.2 × 10⁻¹⁵ N. Since the electron is negative, the force is opposite to the field direction — i.e., downward. The force direction is opposite to E for negative charges.
subtopic: Force on a charge in electric field
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: The magnitude is correct but the direction is wrong. Electrons (negative charge) experience force opposite to the electric field direction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting that negative charges move opposite to the field

elimination_hints.hint: The direction is correct (downward) but the magnitude is halved. F = eE = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ × 2×10⁴ = 3.2×10⁻¹⁵ N, not 1.6.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error in force calculation

elimination_hints.hint: The electric force on a charge always acts along the electric field (or opposite to it for negative charges), never perpendicular.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing electric force with magnetic force (which is perpendicular)

question_text: The electric field inside a uniformly charged thin spherical shell is:
explanation: By Gauss's law, choosing a Gaussian surface inside the shell: the enclosed charge is zero (all charge is on the shell). Therefore, ∮E·dA = q_enc/ε₀ = 0, giving E = 0 everywhere inside. This is a fundamental result — a uniformly charged shell creates no field in its interior.
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: kQ/r², where r is distance from centre
Proportional to r (increases linearly from centre)

elimination_hints.hint: kQ/R² is the field at the surface of the shell (r = R), not inside it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the surface field value for interior points

elimination_hints.hint: kQ/r² is the field outside the shell (r > R). Inside (r < R), the enclosed charge is zero.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the outside field formula with the interior

elimination_hints.hint: Linear increase with r describes the field inside a uniformly charged solid sphere, not a hollow shell.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing solid sphere with hollow shell

question_text: In a region where the electric field is uniform, the field lines are:
explanation: In a uniform electric field, the magnitude and direction of E are the same everywhere. Therefore, field lines must be equidistant (same density = same magnitude) and parallel (same direction). They are straight lines with equal spacing.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Converging lines indicate increasing field strength in that direction, which contradicts the definition of a uniform field.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing uniform field with the field near a negative charge

elimination_hints.hint: Diverging lines indicate a positive point charge source. Uniform field doesn't diverge.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing uniform field with radial field

elimination_hints.hint: Curved lines with varying spacing represent a non-uniform field (like near a charge or dipole).

question_text: The electric field on the axis of a uniformly charged disc of radius R and surface charge density σ, at a distance x from the centre (where x << R), is approximately:
explanation: The exact field on the axis of a charged disc is E = (σ/2ε₀)[1 − x/√(x² + R²)]. When x << R: √(x² + R²) ≈ R, so E ≈ (σ/2ε₀)[1 − x/R] ≈ σ/2ε₀ (since x/R → 0). This is the same as the field due to an infinite plane sheet of charge, which makes sense — close to the disc, it looks like an infinite plane.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: σ/ε₀ is the field between two infinite parallel plates (complete enclosure), not on one side of a single disc.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing single plane field with two-plate field

elimination_hints.hint: This expression goes to zero as x → 0, but the field near an infinite plane is constant (σ/2ε₀), not zero.
The field at any finite distance from a charged disc is non-zero. It approaches σ/2ε₀ as x → 0.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the field is zero on the surface of the disc

question_text: A proton is released from rest in a uniform electric field of magnitude 8 × 10⁴ V/m. The acceleration of the proton is (mass of proton = 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ kg):
explanation: Force on proton: F = eE = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 8 × 10⁴ = 1.28 × 10⁻¹⁴ N. Acceleration: a = F/m = 1.28 × 10⁻¹⁴ / 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ = 7.66 × 10¹² m/s². The proton accelerates in the direction of the field (since it's positive).
subtopic: Motion of charges in electric fields
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: 10¹⁰ is two orders of magnitude too small. Check: 10⁻¹⁴/10⁻²⁷ = 10¹³, so the order should be ~10¹².
elimination_hints.misconception: Exponent arithmetic error

elimination_hints.hint: 1.28 × 10⁻¹⁴ N is the force, not the acceleration. You need to divide by mass.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reporting force instead of acceleration

elimination_hints.hint: 10¹⁵ is three orders too large — possibly from using electron mass instead of proton mass.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using electron mass instead of proton mass

question_text: The electric field at a point on the equatorial line of a short electric dipole at distance r (r >> 2a) from the centre is E₁. If the point is now moved to the same distance r on the axial line, the electric field E₂ is:
explanation: For a short dipole at large distance r: Equatorial field E₁ = kp/r³. Axial field E₂ = 2kp/r³. Therefore E₂ = 2E₁. The axial field is always twice the equatorial field at the same distance from a dipole.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: E₁/2 reverses the ratio. The axial field is stronger than equatorial, not weaker.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inverting the axial-to-equatorial ratio

elimination_hints.hint: Equal fields would mean the axial and equatorial expressions are the same, but E_axial has a factor of 2 that E_equatorial doesn't.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming the field is the same in all directions at a given r

elimination_hints.hint: A factor of 4 would apply if the distance were halved (since E ∝ 1/r³), not for a change from equatorial to axial at the same r.

question_text: An electric dipole is placed in a non-uniform electric field. The dipole will experience:
explanation: In a non-uniform field, the forces on the two charges of the dipole are unequal (since E differs at their positions). This produces both a net torque (trying to align the dipole with the field) AND a net translational force (since F₊ ≠ F₋). In contrast, a uniform field produces only torque (no net force). The dipole moves toward the region of stronger field.
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Both a net force and a net torque
Only a net torque, no net force
Only a net force, no net torque
Neither net force nor net torque

elimination_hints.hint: 'Only torque, no force' is true for a UNIFORM field. In a non-uniform field, the unequal forces on the two charges create a net translational force as well.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying the uniform-field result to a non-uniform field

elimination_hints.hint: The torque is always present (unless the dipole is already aligned with the field). There's no scenario where there's force but no torque in a non-uniform field.
A dipole in any external field (uniform or not) experiences at least a torque (unless aligned). In non-uniform fields, it also has a net force.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking a dipole doesn't interact with external fields

question_text: The electric field inside a uniformly charged solid non-conducting sphere of radius R and total charge Q, at a distance r from the centre (r < R), is:
explanation: Using Gauss's law with a spherical Gaussian surface of radius r inside the sphere: q_enc = Q(r³/R³) (charge enclosed proportional to volume). By Gauss's law: E × 4πr² = Q(r³/R³)/ε₀. Solving: E = Qr/(4πε₀R³) = kQr/R³. The field increases linearly from centre to surface.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: kQ/r² is the field OUTSIDE the sphere (r > R). Inside, only the enclosed charge contributes, which is less than Q.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using total charge Q instead of enclosed charge for interior points

elimination_hints.hint: kQ/R² is the field at the surface (r = R). Inside, the field varies linearly with r, it's not constant.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using surface value for all interior points

elimination_hints.hint: Zero field inside applies to a conducting sphere or a hollow shell, not a solid non-conducting sphere with uniform volume charge.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing non-conducting solid sphere with conducting sphere or shell

question_text: The torque experienced by an electric dipole of moment p placed in a uniform electric field E at angle θ to the field is maximum when θ equals:
explanation: Torque τ = pE sinθ. This is maximum when sinθ = 1, i.e., θ = 90°. When the dipole is perpendicular to the field, the torque is maximum (τ_max = pE). At θ = 0° (aligned) or θ = 180° (anti-aligned), the torque is zero.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: At θ = 0°, the dipole is aligned with the field and sin0° = 0, so the torque is zero (stable equilibrium).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing equilibrium position with maximum torque position

elimination_hints.hint: At θ = 180°, sin180° = 0, so the torque is again zero (unstable equilibrium). Maximum torque requires sinθ = 1.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing unstable equilibrium with maximum torque

elimination_hints.hint: At 45°, sin45° = 1/√2, which gives τ = pE/√2 — this is about 71% of maximum, not the maximum itself.

question_text: The electric field due to an infinitely long straight wire with uniform linear charge density λ at perpendicular distance r from the wire is:
explanation: Using Gauss's law with a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius r and length l: E × 2πrl = λl/ε₀. Solving: E = λ/(2πε₀r). The field is directed radially outward (for positive λ) and falls off as 1/r — slower than the 1/r² of a point charge.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: This has 1/r² dependence (like a point charge) and uses 4πε₀ in the denominator. The wire field has 1/r dependence and 2πε₀.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying point charge formula to a line charge

elimination_hints.hint: λ/ε₀r is missing the factor of 2π from the cylindrical Gaussian surface area (2πrl).
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrect Gaussian surface area calculation

elimination_hints.hint: 4πε₀r uses the wrong geometric factor. For cylindrical symmetry, the surface area gives 2πrl, leading to 2πε₀ in the denominator.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the spherical factor 4πε₀ for a cylindrical problem

question_text:
Assertion (A): The electric field at a point on the surface of a charged conductor is perpendicular to the surface.
Reason (R): If there were a tangential component of the field, the free charges on the conductor surface would move, contradicting electrostatic equilibrium.
explanation: Both A and R are correct, and R is the correct explanation of A. In electrostatic equilibrium, charges on a conductor are stationary. If E had a tangential component at the surface, it would exert a force on the free charges, causing them to move — violating the assumption of static equilibrium. Therefore, E must be perpendicular to the surface.
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains A — the absence of tangential force (which would move charges) is exactly why the field must be perpendicular.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising the causal connection between charge motion and field direction

elimination_hints.hint: R is factually correct — free charges would indeed move if there were a tangential field component.
A is correct — electric field is always perpendicular to a conductor surface in electrostatic equilibrium.

question_text:
Assertion (A): The density of electric field lines is proportional to the magnitude of the electric field at that point.
Reason (R): Electric field lines are drawn such that the number of lines per unit cross-sectional area is proportional to E.
explanation: Both A and R are correct, and R correctly explains A. By convention, field lines are drawn so that the number of lines crossing a unit area perpendicular to the lines equals the field magnitude. This is why regions of closely spaced lines have stronger fields, and widely spaced lines indicate weaker fields.
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: R is precisely the convention that makes A true — the density of field lines represents field strength because they are drawn that way. R explains A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing that R is the definition that makes A true

elimination_hints.hint: R is correct — this is the standard convention for drawing field lines in physics.
A is correct — field line density does represent field magnitude. This is how field maps work.

question_text: The electric field between two large parallel plates carrying equal and opposite surface charge densities +σ and −σ is:
explanation: Each plate produces a field σ/(2ε₀). Between the plates, both fields point in the same direction (from + to − plate), so they add: E = σ/(2ε₀) + σ/(2ε₀) = σ/ε₀. Outside the plates, the fields cancel. This is the basis of a parallel plate capacitor.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: σ/(2ε₀) is the field due to a single infinite plate. Between two oppositely charged plates, both fields add up.
elimination_hints.misconception: Considering only one plate instead of both

elimination_hints.hint: 2σ/ε₀ would arise from incorrectly doubling the combined field. Each plate contributes σ/(2ε₀), and the total is σ/ε₀.
elimination_hints.misconception: Double-counting the field contributions

elimination_hints.hint: Zero field exists outside the plates (fields cancel), not between them. Between the plates, the fields reinforce.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the region between the plates with the region outside

question_text: A charge q is placed at the centre of a cube. The electric flux through one face of the cube is:
explanation: By Gauss's law, total flux through the cube = q/ε₀. By symmetry, the cube has 6 identical faces, so flux through each face = q/(6ε₀). This is a standard application of Gauss's law using symmetry arguments.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: q/ε₀ is the total flux through all 6 faces, not just one face.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using total flux instead of dividing by the number of faces

elimination_hints.hint: Dividing by 2 suggests only 2 faces, but a cube has 6 symmetric faces.
elimination_hints.misconception: Dividing by 2 instead of 6

elimination_hints.hint: Dividing by 4 suggests 4 faces. A cube has 6 faces, each receiving an equal share of flux.

question_text: The diagram shows electric field line patterns near different charge configurations. Which pattern correctly represents the field lines of a single isolated positive charge?
image_alt: Four field line patterns: (A) lines converging inward to a point, (B) straight radial lines going outward from a point, (C) closed circular loops around a point, (D) parallel straight lines
explanation: A single positive charge produces radial field lines pointing outward in all directions. Pattern B (radial outward) is correct. Pattern A shows a negative charge (lines inward). Pattern C shows magnetic field lines (closed loops). Pattern D shows a uniform field.
subtopic: Field lines of simple charge distributions
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Lines converging inward represent the field of a negative charge, not positive. Positive charges are sources, not sinks.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing positive and negative charge field patterns

elimination_hints.hint: Electric field lines never form closed loops — that is a property of magnetic field lines.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing electric field lines with magnetic field lines

elimination_hints.hint: Parallel lines represent a uniform field (like between capacitor plates), not the radial field of a point charge.

question_text: The diagram shows the electric field lines between two charges. The field lines start from a charge on the left and end on a charge on the right, with the lines curving outward between them. Which of the following correctly describes the two charges?
image_alt: Field lines originating from the left charge (diverging outward) and terminating on the right charge (converging inward), with curved paths between them
explanation: Field lines originate from positive charges and terminate on negative charges. Since lines start from the left charge, it is positive. Since they end on the right charge, it is negative. The charges form a dipole.
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Left is positive, right is negative
Left is negative, right is positive

elimination_hints.hint: If both were positive, field lines would diverge from both charges and never terminate on either — they would extend to infinity.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing that field lines terminate on negative charges

elimination_hints.hint: If both were negative, field lines would converge on both and originate from infinity — they wouldn't start from either charge.
This reverses the convention. Lines emerge from positive charges (where they start) and enter negative charges (where they end).
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the start/end convention for field lines

question_text: The figure shows the variation of electric field E with distance r from the centre of a charged sphere. The graph shows E increasing linearly from r = 0 to r = R, then decreasing as 1/r² for r > R. This graph represents the electric field of:
image_alt: Graph of E vs r: E rises linearly from 0 to a maximum at r = R, then falls as 1/r² for r > R. The peak is at r = R.
explanation: Inside a uniformly charged non-conducting solid sphere: E = kQr/R³ (linear increase). Outside: E = kQ/r² (inverse square decrease). Maximum occurs at the surface (r = R). This is distinctly different from a conducting sphere (E = 0 for r < R) or a shell (E = 0 for r < R).
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: A thin uniformly charged spherical shell
A point charge at the origin
A uniformly charged non-conducting solid sphere

elimination_hints.hint: Inside a conducting sphere, E = 0 (no field). The graph shows E increasing linearly from centre, which means charge is distributed throughout the volume.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing conducting sphere with non-conducting solid sphere

elimination_hints.hint: Inside a thin shell, E = 0 (no enclosed charge). The graph shows non-zero E inside, indicating volume charge distribution.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing shell with solid sphere

elimination_hints.hint: A point charge has E = kq/r² everywhere (no linear region). The graph's linear rise indicates distributed charge.

question_text: The figure shows equipotential surfaces (V₁ > V₂ > V₃) that are concentric circles, with V₁ being the innermost. The spacing between adjacent surfaces decreases as we move outward. What can be concluded about the electric field?
image_alt: Three concentric circular equipotential surfaces. V₁ (innermost) > V₂ > V₃ (outermost). The gap between V₁ and V₂ is larger than between V₂ and V₃.
explanation: E = −dV/dr, so the field is stronger where equipotential surfaces are closer together. Since spacing decreases outward, the field increases outward. The field is directed radially inward (from high V to low V means inward toward centre — wait, V₁ > V₂ > V₃ with V₁ innermost means potential decreases outward, so E points outward (from high to low potential). But the spacing DECREASES outward, meaning E INCREASES. Actually, this contradicts a normal positive point charge (where E decreases). This pattern could represent the interior of a non-conducting charged sphere where E increases linearly outward.
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: The field is directed radially outward and increases with distance from the centre
The field is directed radially inward and decreases with distance
The field is uniform throughout the region
The field is directed radially outward and decreases with distance from the centre

elimination_hints.hint: Since V decreases outward (V₁ > V₂ > V₃), E = −dV/dr points outward (in the direction of decreasing V), not inward.
elimination_hints.misconception: Getting the direction of E wrong relative to the potential gradient

elimination_hints.hint: In a uniform field, equipotential surfaces are equally spaced planes. Here, the spacing varies, indicating a non-uniform field.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking unequal spacing can represent a uniform field

elimination_hints.hint: If E decreased with distance, the equipotential surfaces would get farther apart (not closer), since |ΔV| = E·Δr.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing closer spacing with weaker field instead of stronger field

question_text:
Assertion (A): Electric field lines are always perpendicular to equipotential surfaces.
Reason (R): No work is done in moving a charge along an equipotential surface.
explanation: Both A and R are correct, and R explains A. On an equipotential surface, V is constant, so dV = 0. Work done dW = q·dV = 0. Since W = F·ds·cosθ = qE·ds·cosθ = 0 and q ≠ 0, ds ≠ 0, E ≠ 0, we need cosθ = 0, meaning θ = 90°. Therefore E is perpendicular to the surface.
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: R is correct — by definition, potential is constant on an equipotential surface, so work done in moving a charge along it is zero.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking work is done along equipotential surfaces

elimination_hints.hint: A is correct — it is a standard result that E is perpendicular to equipotential surfaces. This follows directly from E = −∇V.
Read carefully — this option says the same as B. Both state R correctly explains A, which is true.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Electrostatic field lines do not form closed loops.
Reason (R): The electrostatic field is conservative in nature.
explanation: Both A and R are correct, and R correctly explains A. A conservative field has zero circulation: ∮E·dl = 0. If field lines formed closed loops, this integral would be non-zero (positive work done around the loop), contradicting the conservative nature. Hence, electrostatic field lines are open — they start on positive charges and end on negative charges.
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A

elimination_hints.hint: The conservative nature IS the reason field lines can't form closed loops — zero circulation means no closed paths. R directly explains A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing the connection between conservative fields and open field lines

elimination_hints.hint: R is correct — the electrostatic field is indeed conservative (curl E = 0).
A is correct — electrostatic field lines never form closed loops, unlike magnetic field lines.

question_text:
Match the charge distribution in Column I with the distance dependence of its electric field in Column II:

Column I:
P. Point charge
Q. Infinite line charge
R. Infinite plane sheet
S. Electric dipole (far field)

Column II:
1. E ∝ 1/r
2. E ∝ 1/r²
3. E ∝ 1/r³
4. E is constant (independent of r)
explanation: Point charge: E = kq/r² → P-2. Infinite line charge: E = λ/(2πε₀r) → Q-1. Infinite plane: E = σ/(2ε₀) → R-4 (constant). Dipole far field: E ∝ 1/r³ → S-3.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns 1/r³ to line charge and 1/r to dipole. Line charge has 1/r (slower decay), dipole has 1/r³ (faster decay).
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping line charge and dipole field dependences

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns 1/r to point charge, but E ∝ 1/r² for a point charge (inverse square law).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing point charge field with line charge field

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns 1/r³ to point charge and 1/r² to dipole. The point charge has 1/r² and the dipole has 1/r³.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping point charge and dipole field dependences

question_text:
Match the physical situation in Column I with the correct statement about electric field in Column II:

Column I:
P. Inside a charged conducting sphere
Q. Inside a uniformly charged non-conducting solid sphere
R. Between parallel plates (+σ and −σ)
S. Outside a spherical shell at distance r > R

Column II:
1. E = kQ/r² (same as point charge)
2. E = 0
3. E = σ/ε₀ (uniform)
4. E increases linearly with distance from centre
explanation: Inside a conducting sphere: E = 0 (P-2). Inside a non-conducting solid sphere: E = kQr/R³, linearly increasing (Q-4). Between parallel plates: E = σ/ε₀, uniform (R-3). Outside a shell: E = kQ/r², same as point charge (S-1).
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns kQ/r² to inside a solid sphere, but inside a solid sphere E increases linearly with r, not as 1/r².
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the external field formula for interior points

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns linearly increasing field to inside a conductor. Inside a conductor, E = 0 always (charges reside on the surface).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing conductor with non-conducting solid sphere

elimination_hints.hint: This swaps the field between parallel plates (σ/ε₀) with the solid sphere interior (linear). The plates give uniform field, the solid sphere gives linear.

question_text: Electric field lines always originate from positive charges and terminate on negative charges.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. By convention, electric field lines emerge from positive charges (sources) and end on negative charges (sinks). If no negative charge is present, lines extend to infinity. This is a fundamental property of electrostatic field lines.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: This is a correct and fundamental property of electrostatic field lines — they originate from positive and terminate on negative charges.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking field lines can originate from negative charges or form loops

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable for true/false questions.
This option is not applicable for true/false questions.

question_text: The total electric flux through a closed surface depends on the shape and size of the surface.
explanation: This statement is FALSE. By Gauss's law, the total electric flux through any closed surface Φ = q_enc/ε₀, which depends ONLY on the net charge enclosed, not on the shape or size of the surface. A large sphere and a small cube enclosing the same charge will have the same total flux.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Gauss's law states Φ = q_enc/ε₀. The flux depends only on enclosed charge, not on the surface geometry.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing flux density (which varies over a surface) with total flux (which depends only on enclosed charge)

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable for true/false questions.
This option is not applicable for true/false questions.

question_text: The electric field at a point due to a positive charge is directed away from the charge.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. A positive charge repels a positive test charge. Since the electric field direction is defined as the direction of force on a positive test charge, the field points radially away from a positive source charge.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: The electric field from a positive charge points outward (away). For a negative charge, it points inward (toward the charge).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the field direction of positive and negative charges

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable for true/false questions.
This option is not applicable for true/false questions.

question_text: In a region where the electric field is zero, the electric potential must also be zero.
explanation: This statement is FALSE. E = 0 means there is no potential gradient (dV/dr = 0), so the potential is constant — but that constant need not be zero. Example: inside a charged conducting sphere, E = 0 but V = kQ/R (a non-zero constant).
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Zero field means the potential is constant, not necessarily zero. Inside a charged conducting shell, E = 0 but V = kQ/R ≠ 0.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing zero field (constant potential) with zero potential

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable for true/false questions.
This option is not applicable for true/false questions.

question_text: The electric flux through a closed surface enclosing a charge of 5 μC is ______ N·m²/C. (Given: ε₀ = 8.85 × 10⁻¹² C²/N·m²)
explanation: By Gauss's law, Φ = q/ε₀ = 5 × 10⁻⁶ / 8.85 × 10⁻¹² = 5.65 × 10⁵ N·m²/C. This is a direct application of Gauss's law — the flux depends only on the enclosed charge.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: 10³ would result from dividing the exponents incorrectly. 10⁻⁶/10⁻¹² = 10⁶, giving ~5.65 × 10⁵.
elimination_hints.misconception: Exponent arithmetic error

elimination_hints.hint: 10⁷ is two orders too high. Check: 5/8.85 ≈ 0.565, and 10⁻⁶/10⁻¹² = 10⁶, so the answer is ~5.65 × 10⁵.
A negative exponent would mean extremely small flux, which is unreasonable for a microcoulomb charge.
elimination_hints.misconception: Sign error in the exponent calculation

question_text: An electric dipole of moment 4 × 10⁻⁹ C·m is placed in a uniform electric field of 5 × 10⁵ N/C at 30° to the field. The torque on the dipole is ______ N·m.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: 2 × 10⁻³ would result from using sin60° (= √3/2 ≈ 0.866) instead of sin30° (= 0.5), or from forgetting the sin factor.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the wrong angle or cosine instead of sine

elimination_hints.hint: 4 × 10⁻³ results from omitting the sinθ factor entirely (τ = pE without sin30°).
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting the sine factor in the torque formula

elimination_hints.hint: 10⁻⁴ is one order too small, likely from an exponent error: 10⁻⁹ × 10⁵ = 10⁻⁴ before the factor of 4×5×0.5 = 10.

scenario: A physics laboratory has set up two large parallel metal plates separated by 5 cm. The plates are connected to a 200 V battery, creating a uniform electric field between them. A tiny oil droplet of mass 3.2 × 10⁻¹⁵ kg carrying an unknown charge is introduced between the plates. The droplet is observed to remain stationary, perfectly suspended in mid-air.
question_text: What is the charge on the oil droplet? (Take g = 10 m/s²)
explanation: For the droplet to be stationary, electric force must balance gravity: qE = mg. The electric field E = V/d = 200/0.05 = 4000 V/m. So q = mg/E = 3.2 × 10⁻¹⁵ × 10 / 4000 = 3.2 × 10⁻¹⁴/4000 = 8 × 10⁻¹⁸ C. This is exactly 50 electron charges (50 × 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹), confirming charge quantisation.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C is a single electron charge. The calculation gives q = mg/E = 8 × 10⁻¹⁸ C, which is 50e, not 1e.
elimination_hints.misconception: Guessing the fundamental charge without calculating

elimination_hints.hint: 3.2 × 10⁻¹⁷ is too large by a factor of 4, possibly from using d = 5 cm as 0.5 m instead of 0.05 m.
elimination_hints.misconception: Unit conversion error for plate separation

elimination_hints.hint: 4 × 10⁻¹⁵ would result from using E = V (200) instead of E = V/d (4000).
elimination_hints.misconception: Using voltage directly instead of E = V/d

scenario: A research team is studying the electric field inside a cavity carved out of a large uniformly charged non-conducting sphere. The sphere has a uniform volume charge density ρ. A small spherical cavity of radius a is carved out such that its centre is at a distance d from the centre of the large sphere (d > a, so the cavity is entirely inside the sphere). Using the principle of superposition, the team models the cavity as the original sphere plus a sphere of charge density −ρ filling the cavity.
question_text: What can the team conclude about the electric field inside the cavity?
explanation: By superposition, E_inside cavity = E_full sphere − E_small sphere. For the full sphere at position r from its centre: E₁ = ρr/(3ε₀). For the negative sphere centred at distance d: E₂ = −ρr'/(3ε₀) where r' is measured from the cavity centre. The net field: E = ρ(r − r')/(3ε₀) = ρd/(3ε₀), where d is the vector from sphere centre to cavity centre. This is uniform inside the cavity — constant magnitude and direction.
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: The field is zero everywhere inside the cavity
The field is uniform (constant magnitude and direction) inside the cavity
The field increases linearly from the cavity centre
The field is radially symmetric about the cavity centre

elimination_hints.hint: Zero field inside a cavity occurs only when the cavity is at the centre of the sphere (d = 0). For an off-centre cavity, the field is non-zero and uniform.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming zero field inside any cavity regardless of its position

elimination_hints.hint: Linear increase with distance describes the field inside a uniformly charged solid sphere, not inside a cavity within such a sphere.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the cavity field with the solid sphere field

elimination_hints.hint: The cavity breaks the spherical symmetry. The resulting field is uniform (same everywhere in the cavity), not radially symmetric about the cavity centre.

question_text: Arrange the following steps in the correct order for applying Gauss's law to find the electric field due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet:

items.text: Choose a cylindrical Gaussian surface with its axis perpendicular to the sheet
Note that flux through curved surface is zero (E ⊥ dA) and flux through each flat face is EA

explanation: Step 1: Choose the appropriate Gaussian surface (cylinder). Step 2: State Gauss's law. Step 3: Evaluate the surface integral using symmetry. Step 4: Find the enclosed charge. Step 5: Solve for E. This systematic approach works for all Gauss's law problems.
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Evaluating the surface integral (R) before writing Gauss's law (Q) is out of order. You must state the law before applying it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Skipping the formal statement of the law

elimination_hints.hint: Writing Gauss's law before choosing the Gaussian surface doesn't make physical sense — you need to define the surface first.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising that the surface must be chosen first

elimination_hints.hint: This puts enclosed charge (S) before evaluating the surface integral (R). You need to simplify the left side before computing the right side to solve for E.

question_text: Arrange the following charge distributions in order of increasing rate at which their electric field decreases with distance r (from slowest decrease to fastest decrease):

items.text: Infinite plane sheet of charge (E ∝ r⁰)

explanation: Slowest decrease: infinite plane (E = constant, no decrease at all). Then line charge (1/r). Then point charge (1/r²). Fastest decrease: dipole (1/r³). The rate of decrease correlates with the dimensionality: 2D sheet → 1D line → 0D point → dipole (which partially cancels).
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: This is the reverse order (fastest to slowest). The question asks for slowest decrease to fastest decrease.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the order asked for

elimination_hints.hint: This places point charge (1/r²) before line charge (1/r). But 1/r decreases slower than 1/r², so line charge should come before point charge.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing which power law decreases faster

elimination_hints.hint: This places line charge before plane sheet. The plane sheet has constant E (no decrease at all), so it must come first.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting that the infinite plane field is constant
